data.title: Nano Labs: Ambitious Binance Coin Bet Transforms Chip Maker Into Crypto Play
data.description:
Nano Labs Ltd. (NASDAQ:NA) is a hybrid technology-crypto company that develops ASIC hardware and blockchain infrastructure. NA also has a business segment that accumulates strategic digital assets (i.e., cryptocurrencies). They also developed high-throughput computing chips like the Cuckoo 3.0 and advanced architectures such as FPU 3.0. However, their recent push into the crypto space is noteworthy. Their formal adoption of Bitcoin as a reserve asset, acquisition of over 120,000 BNB tokens (BNB-USD), and planned launch of a licensed stablecoin platform in Hong Kong shift NA into more of a “crypto” play at this point. Still, I feel the stock is a bit too risky to warrant a bullish rating, which is why I settle on a “Hold” at these levels.

Crypto Tech

Nano Labs is technically a fabless integrated circuit (IC) design firm that develops computing and application-specific chips, as well as software solutions for its hardware. They were founded back in 2019, with an IPO in 2022, and they’re currently headquartered in Hangzhou, China. Additionally, the company defines its value proposition through accumulating digital asset reserves, mainly Bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Today, NA’s portfolio includes a range of semiconductor products, such as high-throughput computing (HTC) chips, part of the Cuckoo series, high-performance computing (HPC) chips, vision processing chips, and smart network interface cards (NICs). It’s worth mentioning that their Cuckoo series Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs) compete with traditional GPUs.

Additionally, the company offers integrated software solutions like firmware, control programs, tools for monitoring performance, and blockchain-specific systems to operate and enhance the hardware. In total, these technology packages are intended for enterprise and individual customers. NA’s Enterprise clients are typically mining farms, cloud computing providers, or AI infrastructure operators. Whereas individual customers often include smaller-scale users, such as independent crypto miners.

Having said that, NA launched in December 2024 an upgraded computing device with the Cuckoo 3.0 chip. It’s their latest-generation ASIC chip for HTC. However, it’s particularly useful for mining cryptocurrencies or other Web3 infrastructure workloads. This chip version offers better computing power and efficiency, with a single-core performance of 1.2 gigahashes per second (GH/s) and energy efficiency of 0.14 watts per megahash (W/MHash). To give you an idea, this actually represents an 83% improvement compared to the previous generation, which had more energy consumption and reached 0.79 W/MHash.

Chip Differentiators And Crypto Strategy

Moreover, NA’s Cuckoo chips are built for the V2 series. These chips are intended for home and small-scale miners that feature silent operation and high performance with low energy consumption. NA also released the floating-point unit (FPU) 3.0 architecture built as an ASIC and optimized for specific tasks like AI inference or blockchain processing. The key innovation is the integration of 3D-stacked DRAM for bandwidth increment (up to 24 TB/s) and reduced latency and power consumption. They achieved these efficiencies thanks to the shortening distances between memory and logic using a smart network-on-chip.

Essentially, this gives NA’s chip better internal connections and overall improves its competitiveness in the ASIC market for crypto mining. Furthermore, NA’s latest earnings call mentioned they are officially establishing their Bitcoin value investment as a business unit. Their idea is to leverage the global trend of increased adoption of cryptocurrencies, especially Bitcoin.

Currently, NA has a segment focused on holding, managing, and expanding its BTC assets. This formal status requires board-level oversight and controls integrated into public filings, as crypto is heavily regulated in China. As of recently, NA holds approximately 400 BTC, valued at around $40 million. On top of that, NA expanded its crypto holdings by buying 120,000 Binance (BNB) tokens. That amounts to a combined total (BTC and BNB) of approximately $160 million.

But more interestingly, NA is not just investing in BNB. Instead, it seems this ecosystem is more of a strategic play for the company as a whole. NA’s management is keen on positioning itself as a major long-term holder and financial backer of the Binance ecosystem. Although in the long run they aim to control 5%–10% of BNB’s circulating supply, which should give them a material stake in that ecosystem. It’s also an extremely ambitious target since BNB’s market cap is approximately $106.7 billion, so just 5% of that value would be equivalent to roughly $5.3 billion.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, NA trades at a $139.2 million market cap, so it remains a relatively small company nonetheless. Their recent annual report shows that their balance sheet holds $4.5 million in cash and $33.8 million in cryptocurrencies (by year-end 2024). NA also reported almost $27.0 million in financial debt (aside from other regular operating liabilities), suggesting it’s relatively indebted already. And in June 2025, they announced a whopping $500 million note deal to continue financing future crypto purchases.

A more recent update shows their crypto holdings amount to approximately $160.0 million as of July 2025. And I do have to give them credit for their latest BNB buy, since their price average is around $672.45, and BNB is trading at about $767.04 already. So, this strategy has worked out for NA so far, but we’ll have to wait and see if, in the long run, this pays off.

It’s also worth mentioning that NA is partnering with Orbiter Finance to launch a stablecoin called NBNB.io. They’re aiming for a potential Q4 2025 release, and NA is applying for licenses in Hong Kong to issue these fiat-referenced stablecoins related to the Hong Kong dollar (HKD) and offshore Chinese Yuan (RMB). Additionally, NA intends to build a technical infrastructure for stablecoins compatible with the BTS and BNB chains. With stablecoins as core Web3 infrastructure, NA aims to integrate them into cross-chain products, including NBNB.io, via their partner Orbiter Finance.

It’s true that these moves can potentially add a new revenue stream to the chip design and crypto reserves businesses. However, I remain skeptical about their actual competitive prospects with their new stablecoin project. After all, USDT and USDC are well-entrenched and mass-adopted stablecoins in crypto. Those stablecoin alternatives are also regulated, and you can even bet on USDC adoption through Circle Internet Group (CRCL). So, in that sense, I’m not as optimistic about this stablecoin strategic direction that NA’s management is now focusing on.

Yet, this stablecoin bet also seems similar to their incredibly ambitious aim of controlling 5%–10% of BNB’s circulating supply. It sounds great on paper, but in practice, it’s extremely difficult to achieve. After all, 5%-10% of BNB would amount to several billions of dollars in capital investments, which NA simply doesn’t have at this point. Ostensibly, they’ll finance their purchases with debt (or more stock issuance), but I’m not even sure they can access such a huge amount of loans. NA remains a relatively small microcap for all intents and purposes, so I doubt lenders will simply loan those amounts to an otherwise unprofitable company. Likewise, their near-term revenue prospects seem unremarkable as well. In fact, in 2024, they only generated about $5.6 million in total revenues.

Nonetheless, it does show that NA is quickly becoming a leveraged bet in crypto, particularly in BTC and BNB. So, I anticipate the stock will become increasingly volatile as NA’s leverage factor increases with its crypto holdings. On the bull case, BNB and BTC both rally, and NA benefits greatly from such unrealized gains. But the flip side is equally true, and the downside could be substantial and swift since crypto is already notably volatile. If you add to that volatility leverage (like NA is doing), then it’s clear that new investors are taking a substantial risk with the stock.

I also estimate they burned through $24.5 million in 2024. Note that I got that figure by simply adding their cash flows from operations and CAPEX during that period (excluding cryptocurrency purchases). In contrast, we know they had just about $4.5 million in cash (year-end 2024) and about $160 million in cryptocurrencies (July 2025). So, if we combine those figures, it suggests a cash runway of around 6.7 years, which would seem extremely healthy. However, we know they don’t intend to sell their crypto holdings, meaning that their actual runway is probably much shorter.

If anything, management wants to continue investing in BTC and BNB. Thus, it’s clear to me that NA will likely keep on piling on more debt or issuing more stock to finance its operations and cryptocurrency purchases. And all of this paints a picture that seems somewhat reckless to me, which is why I can’t give them a bullish rating. I also believe their high-risk, high-reward strategy could pay off massively if BTC and BNB rally and if their stablecoin is a success. That’s why I feel a “Hold” rating at these levels seems fair.

Conclusion: Sidelines For Now

Overall, I get the impression that NA’s ambitions are extremely high and maybe a bit unrealistic. They want to control a sizeable stake in the huge BNB ecosystem while also launching a stablecoin contender against USDT or USDC, which are well-entrenched alternatives. These two initiatives could definitely pay off significantly, but I remain skeptical since NA’s financial resources are clearly a very limiting factor. So far, NA has resorted to taking on debt and issuing shares to finance these goals, but this does increase its risk factor proportionally. Hence, I think NA is now a very high-risk, high-reward play in crypto rather than a straight-up miner. In my view, I believe this is simply too speculative to warrant a bullish rating, which is why I lean towards a “Hold” for now.

data.title: Alphabet: Still Undervalued, Caution Warranted
data.description:
Alphabet (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) has outperformed since we announced it as the only FAANG company we're investing more in. The company not only has an incredibly strong core business, still growing in the double digits, but it also has leading auxiliary businesses ranging from Google Cloud to Waymo. As we'll see throughout this article, the company's capex spending gives reason to pause, but overall the company is a valuable investment.

Alphabet's Financial Results

Alphabet has managed to improve its financials substantially, with 14% YoY revenue growth.

The company has grown its revenue from almost $85 billion to more than $96 billion. The company has grown R&D expenses at 16% YoY, with total cost and expenses along with income matching that 14% growth. The company managed 19% net income growth and massive 22% diluted EPS growth, massive scale for a company earning almost $400 billion in annual revenue.

Google Breakdown

Regardless of all of the noise around Waymo, Artificial Intelligence, and more, the core of the company's portfolio is one thing. Google.

The company has managed to grow this business from just under $74 billion to more than $82 billion in annual revenue. That makes the business the most important for the company. Google Search continues to outperform, with 12% YoY revenue growth. The company has also managed to do incredibly well with YouTube Ads, up 13% YoY.

In the age of the streaming wars, YouTube remains the largest streaming and video asset globally. Google Network, or the company's ad network remains flat, while the company's subscription business is a bright spot with 20% YoY growth. The company managed to translate growth to its bottom line maintaining a 40.1% operating margin with more than $33 billion in income.

Google Cloud Performance

A growing division for the company and the source of most of its capex is the company's cloud division.

Here the company managed to accelerate growth to 32% top line YoY revenue growth up from 29% the prior year. The company also managed to almost double operating margins at the same time, pushing the business to more than $10 billion in annual operating income, a strong diver to the company's bottom line performance.

While demand for cloud remains strong, the company's capital spending and how much it's investing remains a concern.

Alphabet's Capital Spending

Alphabet is massively ramping up its capital spending for a new era of artificial intelligence and the company is doing it at unprecedented speeds.

The company spent just over $30 billion in 2022 capital expenditures and it's now guiding for more than $85 billion in 2025 capital expenditures. That massive growth represents a $55 billion decline in FCF. The company is lucky that it's financially strong enough that it can afford to do this, but at a $2.3 trillion that's a lot of capital that could be funding shareholder returns.

The company's FCF yield in the most recent quarter dropped to <1% as its TTM FCF has started to take a hit as well from this spending. YoY 4% increase in net cash from operating activities was dwarfed by a 70% increase in capital expenditures. A chunk of this is growing demand for its cloud which is fine, but much of it is also internal.

The company is guiding for increased capital spending going into 2026 as well. However, there is a bright spot here as the company ramps up spending on its TPUs. That ramped up spending will potentially enable the company to spend less on Nvidia's (NVDA) expensive GPUs, saving on the margins that the company is providing there.

Alphabet's Shareholder Returns

Alphabet has worked to consistently reduce its shares outstanding over the past decade after starting its shareholder return policy.

The company has started a dividend policy, albeit an incredibly modest one of roughly 0.4%. The company continues to have a substantial amount of cash and marketable securities on hand, and it announced a $70 billion buyback policy last year. How long the company can sustain buybacks depends on leftover FCF after the company's capital spending.

However, at least that capital spending is continuing to provide double-digit shareholder returns. The growth in subscriptions with the company's new AI subscriptions is also evident in the company's 20% revenue growth YoY. Whether the company runs away with spending too much capex or not remains to be seen.

The commitment to shareholder returns and share buybacks though shows a strong commitment to shareholders.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Alphabet is facing an existential threat to its business, and as seen above search is still the core. Our view is that ChatGPT etc. aren't competitive with human results + artificial intelligence, and competitors don't have the ad business to compete with Google being free. Still a successful search competitor could hurt future returns.

Conclusion

Alphabet remains undervalued on the basis of its almost $100 billion in quarterly revenue growing at double-digits and the company's ability to move that to its bottom line. The company has modest shareholder returns on the basis of its continued massive investments into the business but its subscription and cloud growth show its business strength.

However, there is still some concern here. The company's massive spending is pushing its FCF yield below 1%, and every other company is spending similarly heavily. There's no guarantee that the AI wave pans out the way these companies expect, to some extent, Google is spending because it's afraid of its core business under attack.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

data.title: NerdWallet Is Becoming A Cash Flow Powerhouse
data.description:
In the rubble of fintech's post-2021 comedown, most investors still see NerdWallet (NRDS) as a glorified affiliate network - one that relies on SEO and content marketing to drive clicks and revenue.

In some respects, this is a fair assessment, as the company does generate high-quality financial content that helps people make better decisions in numerous financial categories, like credit cards, bank accounts, insurance, mortgages, and more.

In return, financial partners pay through the nose for leads and clicks that the NRDS generates.

It's a smart, simple business model.

However, in the last few quarters, NRDS has begun looking at itself more strategically. While the company's financial content engine remains robust, the firm doesn't capture nearly as much LTV as the partners to which it sells leads.

This is changing. In recent quarters, NRDS has verticalized the company, acquiring downstream loan, credit, and financial marketplaces that allow the firm to capture more value from the leads it generates.

At the same time, NRDS has also implemented a sticky app experience, a paid NerdWallet+ plan, and more algorithmically accurate recommendations for products and services to help users optimize their financial lives.

All of these moves have far-reaching implications for NRDS's long-term financials.

NRDS has done well up to this point in building out a high margin content business focused on SEO, but we expect that V2 of NerdWallet will begin to produce material shareholder returns as the company internalizes lead flow and accesses much better unit economics.

For its part, the stock has largely been 'range bound' since IPO in November 2021, despite the progress management has made in growing revenues:

Now, with considerably improved unit economics, incredibly high gross margins, and a strong growth / profitability lever that management can pull whenever they want, we think the company is positioned well to deliver strong shareholder returns going forward.

Today, we'll highlight NRDS's business model, outline the company's valuation - which we think appears attractive - and explain why this retail focused, 'financial guidance' platform looks like a 'Strong Buy'.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start by taking a deeper look at what NRDS does, because we’ve always been impressed with the core business model.

In short, NerdWallet produces retail friendly content about financial products across a number of verticals, including credit cards, bank accounts, insurance products, mortgages, investment accounts, and more.

The company writes content about these product verticals for people in the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada, and Australia.

If you’ve ever googled something like 'best options trading broker USA', or 'best credit card for cashback' you’ve likely seen NRDS content strategy at work.

A huge percentage of the company's traffic comes from organic SEO - that is, Google searches and Bing searches - and in the past, NRDS has been able to monetize this immense traffic by offering links and partnerships with financial product companies across the ecosystem.

For example, let’s say someone googles 'best travel credit card rewards 2025' and ends up on a NerdWallet article comparing top credit cards for travel. In that article, NerdWallet will have placed several links with calls to action, and for every fulfilled referral - that is - for every customer who clicks one of those links and then actually opens a credit card, NRDS receives a hefty payout for the lead.

It’s a business with incredible gross margins, given that the cost of serving this content to the wider financial web is relatively low, and singular investments in content can endure over time, generating strong recurring revenues.

Since IPO, NerdWallet has consistently earned gross profits of more than 90%, which is one of the highest gross margin businesses we’ve ever seen:

This, in turn, gives management a tremendous amount of flexibility when it comes to controlling growth versus profitability. In practice, with margins this high, management can choose how much of that gross profit should be re-invested vs. returned to shareholders. Historically, management has chosen to re-invest almost everything, hence the strong top line growth but breakeven operating profile:

While this is all well and good, re-investing for growth appears to have had diminishing returns.

In response to this, management has recently made a number of strategic moves, including buying smaller financial product marketplaces and integrating them into the company's traffic engine.

As a result, while its business has gotten slightly more complex, NRDS has seen an incredible lift in per-lead LTV, which has re-energized top line growth in recent quarters. You can see this from mid-2024 onwards on the blue TTM revenue chart above.

In fact, in Q1 of this year, Next Door Lending, one of NRDS's recent acquisitions, contributed significantly to the company's organic revenue growth, even in a period where mortgage rates were high:

Banking has continued to perform well, with products like high-yield savings accounts showing durable demand three years removed from a zero-interest rate environment. At the same time, this quarter we saw some green shoots in other areas of the business that have been challenged, such as personal loans, where we saw a return to growth after declining 51% year-over-year in Q4, '24 and mortgages, where our acquisition of Next Door Lending contributed significantly to our 23% year-over-year increase in revenue even as mortgage rates remained elevated.

Fundera and On The Barrelhead, two other marketplaces, have also shored up NRDS's finances in a period of relative cyclical weakness.

Not only that, but these vertical integrations do more than just boost longtail revenue, they also expand NRDS's overall market opportunity, which is key:

Finally, NRDS transformed from a content engine into a 'platform', complete with a mobile app. This provides more personalized financial guidance to people via integration with current accounts and credit information, which allows for more customized recommendations for financial products.

In combination with a premium offering that allows for users to earn higher interest rates at partner banks and institutions', NRDS has done a great job at improving its long-term recurring revenue opportunity.

Right now, analysts are anticipating 15%, 8%, and 4% YoY top line growth over the next three years, which we think appears incredibly low relative to the market opportunity and the new sales opportunities that have come about from the acquisitions:

At the same time, while NRDS already produces positive operating cash flow, we expect these moves to boost NerdWallet's bottom line profitability rapidly. Over the next three years, analysts are currently anticipating EPS to grow to $0.89 by December 2027, but we think this materially undercounts all of the recent strategic moves:

All in all, we think NerdWallet is primed to grow revenues in the high teens YoY over the next few years, while rapidly increasing earnings per share as a result of improving operating leverage and the more economical LTV/CAC ratios.

The future looks bright on an organic basis.

Valuation

Not only that, but we think that NRDS appears to be one of the cheaper companies on the market.

At a base level, NRDS earns roughly $70 million in free cash flow per year, and trades at a market cap of roughly $780 million:

Net out the nearly $100,000,000 in cash on the balance sheet, and you’re left with a single-digit free cash flow multiple for the stock.

From there, if you anticipate even modest increases in revenue and net income, we see the outcome being twofold. First off, in a vacuum, organic growth should lead to higher share prices as stronger business results are reflected linearly. This makes sense. We're anticipating mid-teens top line growth, which should reflect in the share price if the multiple stays stable.

However, with companies that are inflecting on GAAP profitability, we’ve also frequently seen rapid multiple expansion. If companies can convince the market that it's reaching sustained bottom line profitability, then investors will rerate shares higher.

Given NRDS's financial profile, we expect continued organic growth as the company executes its verticalized vision, which should include consistent GAAP bottom line income.

That way, NRDS could see combined growth in the numerator and denominator of the P/E multiple, which would likely drive serious appreciation for shareholders.

Nominally NRDS appears inexpensive for the current results investors are getting from management, but versus where the business could be in the coming years, we think shares are underpriced. This goes double when you consider NRDS's high gross margins, strong product market fit, and improving unit economics.

Overall, between the company's robust prospects and attractive multiple, we think shares look like a 'Strong Buy'.

Risks

Of course, there are risks associated with investing in a stock like NRDS. Here are a couple to be aware of.

While the company has done a good job at trying to in-house its current traffic flow and retain users by getting them to sign up into the company's app experience, NRDS is still downstream from any algorithm changes that Google might make to its search algorithm. Additionally, any AI changes - or changes in user search behavior towards AI - could also have an unknown impact on NerdWallet, given that over 70% of the firm's traffic comes from SEO.

This is an important space to watch, and no doubt the company knows this, but NRDS is - to some degree - at the mercy of larger tech titans.

Additionally, a considerable piece of the investment thesis here assumes both growing revenues and improvements in bottom line profitability. Obviously, NRDS has done a good job growing revenues historically, but it remains to be seen whether or not management will be able to produce significant bottom line profits over the short and medium term. The company's free cash flow is nice, but we’d like to see strong, continued GAAP profitability. Without that, shares may continue to languish.

Summary

Overall, between NerdWallet's incredibly high gross margins, improving unit economics, and overall growth opportunity, we're quite bullish on the firm's organic prospects.

Trading at a sub-10x EV/FCF ratio, we also believe the shares look quite cheap from a nominal perspective, which could also allow for considerable multiple expansion.

While there are some risks, we ultimately believe that most investors should have some NRDS in their portfolio.

Thus, our 'Strong Buy' rating.

Stay safe out there!
